children.name: Oryctolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the European rabbit, ancestor of all domestic rabbit breeds and a keystone species whose burrowing and grazing profoundly shape the ecology of Mediterranean scrublands and grasslands.

children.name: Oryctolagus cuniculus
children.description: The European rabbit is a medium-sized leporid native to the Iberian Peninsula and northwest Africa, now introduced worldwide as a domesticated and feral species. It is the only truly social rabbit, living in warrens of complex underground burrow systems. As a keystone species it engineers landscapes through grazing and burrowing, supporting many predators and scavengers.

children.name: Lepus
children.description: The true hares, the largest genus of Leporidae with around 32 species adapted to open habitats from Arctic tundra to desert, characterised by longer legs, larger ears, and a preference for running over burrowing.

children.name: Lepus europaeus
children.description: The European hare is a large, swift lagomorph of open farmland, grassland and woodland edges across Europe and western Asia, introduced to many other continents. Its golden-brown pelage, black-tipped ears and powerful hind limbs enable speeds exceeding 70 km/h. Solitary and nocturnal, it is famous for its 'mad March hare' boxing displays during the spring breeding season.

children.name: Lepus timidus
children.description: The mountain hare occupies boreal forests, moorland and alpine zones from Ireland and Scotland across northern Eurasia to Japan, reaching Arctic latitudes in Scandinavia and Siberia. Its coat turns white in winter for camouflage against snow, then moults back to blue-grey brown in summer. Populations on the Scottish Highlands and Irish uplands are considered distinct subspecies of conservation concern.

children.name: Lepus americanus
children.description: The snowshoe hare is named for its outsized hind feet that act as snowshoes on soft winter snow across boreal forests of North America. Its coat is rich brown in summer and pure white in winter, tracking photoperiod with a seasonal moult. It is a keystone prey species for the Canada lynx, and the cyclic boom-and-bust of both populations — peaking every 10 years — is one of ecology's most famous predator-prey cycles.

children.name: Lepus arcticus
children.description: The Arctic hare is the largest North American leporid, inhabiting the treeless tundra of northern Canada and Greenland where it endures some of the harshest winters on the planet. Most populations remain white year-round; southerly populations develop a grey-brown summer coat. It forms large aggregations of hundreds to thousands of individuals, a behaviour unusual among hares.

children.name: Lepus capensis
children.description: The Cape hare is among the most widespread of all hares, ranging from southern Africa north through the Sahara, the Arabian Peninsula and into Central Asia. It occupies arid and semi-arid open habitats including deserts, steppes and dry savanna. Its large ears and pale sandy pelage are adaptations to hot, dry environments where it relies on speed and cryptic coloration to evade predators.

children.name: Lepus californicus
children.description: The black-tailed jackrabbit is one of the most recognisable mammals of North American deserts and shrublands, identifiable by its enormous black-tipped ears that radiate excess body heat. It inhabits grasslands, scrublands and agricultural areas of the western and central United States and Mexico. Its population cycles influence many predators including coyotes, golden eagles and rattlesnakes.

children.name: Lepus alleni
children.description: The antelope jackrabbit is the largest North American jackrabbit, inhabiting desert grasslands of Arizona and the Sonoran coast of Mexico. Its extraordinarily large ears — the largest relative to body size of any North American hare — function as thermoregulatory radiators in extreme desert heat. When alarmed it bounds rapidly, flashing its white flanks and rump as a visual signal.

children.name: Lepus brachyurus
children.description: The Japanese hare is endemic to the main islands of Japan, occupying forested mountain slopes and subalpine shrublands. It is a small, compact hare with a short tail — reflected in its name — and a brownish coat that may whiten partially in winter at higher elevations. It is the only native hare in Japan and is important in Japanese folklore.

children.name: Lepus callotis
children.description: The white-sided jackrabbit inhabits high-altitude grasslands and meadows straddling the US–Mexico border in New Mexico and Chihuahua. Its strikingly white flanks, visible when it runs, are thought to serve as an alarm signal to alert conspecifics. It is one of the least-studied North American jackrabbits and is considered Near Threatened due to overgrazing and grassland conversion.

children.name: Lepus castroviejoi
children.description: The broom hare is endemic to the Cantabrian Mountains and adjacent ranges of northwestern Spain, where it inhabits subalpine heathlands and broom scrub above 1,000 metres. It is morphologically similar to the European hare but smaller and genetically distinct, and is currently assessed as Vulnerable on the IUCN Red List. Habitat loss from fire suppression and human development threatens its limited range.

children.name: Lepus comus
children.description: The Yunnan hare is restricted to the plateau and mountain valleys of Yunnan Province in southwestern China, at elevations typically between 1,500 and 3,500 metres. It inhabits open grasslands, shrublands and the margins of cultivated areas. Long considered a subspecies of the Cape hare, it was elevated to full species status based on morphological and genetic evidence.

children.name: Lepus connectens
children.description: The Abyssinian hare is found in the highlands of Ethiopia, typically in open grassland and heathland habitats at elevations above 1,800 metres. It is closely related to the Cape hare and has historically been treated as a subspecies of it. Its relatively restricted range in the Ethiopian Highlands makes it potentially susceptible to habitat degradation and agricultural encroachment.

children.name: Lepus corsicanus
children.description: The Italian hare is native to peninsular Italy and Sicily, having been introduced to Corsica in antiquity, which gave rise to its scientific name. It was long confused with the European hare but is genetically and morphologically distinct, and is assessed as Vulnerable by the IUCN. Competition with and hybridisation from introduced European hares poses a significant conservation threat.

children.name: Lepus fagani
children.description: The Ethiopian highland hare is restricted to the upland grasslands and moorlands of Ethiopia above 2,000 metres elevation. It is a medium-sized hare with grizzled brown-grey fur and relatively large ears. Taxonomically complex, it has at times been subsumed within the Cape hare species complex but is recognised as a distinct species by most modern authorities.

children.name: Lepus flavigularis
children.description: The Tehuantepec jackrabbit is one of the rarest lagomorphs in the world, confined to a narrow strip of coastal dune grass and seasonally flooded savanna on the Isthmus of Tehuantepec in Oaxaca, Mexico. It is assessed as Endangered by the IUCN due to severe habitat loss from agricultural conversion, cattle grazing and human settlement. Its striking yellow-ochre throat patch — flavigularis means 'yellow-throated' — distinguishes it from other Mexican jackrabbits.

children.name: Lepus granatensis
children.description: The Iberian hare is endemic to the Iberian Peninsula and the Balearic island of Majorca, inhabiting scrubland, cereal fields and open woodland up to about 2,000 metres. It is smaller and has a richer rufous colouration than the European hare with which it co-occurs in northern Iberia. The species is a key prey item for the Iberian lynx and Bonelli's eagle.

children.name: Lepus habessinicus
children.description: The Abyssinian hare ranges across the semi-arid lowlands and bushed grasslands of Eritrea, Djibouti, Ethiopia and northern Somalia at lower elevations than the Ethiopian highland hare. It is a pale, sandy-coloured hare well suited to hot arid environments, with notably large ears. It is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN and is locally common in appropriate habitat.

children.name: Lepus hainanus
children.description: The Hainan hare is endemic to Hainan Island, China's largest tropical island in the South China Sea. It inhabits tropical and subtropical forests, scrub and grassland up to about 1,200 metres elevation. The species is assessed as Vulnerable by the IUCN owing to deforestation and hunting pressure on its small island range.

children.name: Lepus insularis
children.description: The black jackrabbit is endemic to Espíritu Santo Island in the Gulf of California, Baja California Sur, Mexico. It is melanistic — mostly black or very dark — an unusual colouration among hares that may relate to volcanic substrate matching on the dark lava fields of the island. It is assessed as Vulnerable by the IUCN due to its extremely limited range.

children.name: Lepus mandshuricus
children.description: The Manchurian hare inhabits the temperate mixed and deciduous forests of northeastern China, the Russian Far East and northern Korea, a distinctly forested habitat unusual for the genus. It is a small to medium-sized hare with dark grizzled brown fur and relatively short ears adapted to its forest lifestyle. Unlike most hares it shelters under dense vegetation rather than relying solely on speed to escape predators.

children.name: Lepus microtis
children.description: The African savanna hare is the most widespread hare in sub-Saharan Africa, occurring from Senegal east to Ethiopia and south to South Africa in open savanna, grassland and semi-arid scrub. Its relatively small ears compared to other hares in hot environments are reflected in its name — microtis means 'small-eared'. It is one of the most abundant and least threatened of all African mammals.

children.name: Lepus nigricollis
children.description: The Indian hare is widespread across South Asia from Pakistan and India to Sri Lanka, inhabiting scrub forest, grassland, agricultural land and coastal areas. Its black-nape patch — nigricollis means 'black-necked' — distinguishes it from related species. It has been introduced to the Andaman and Nicobar Islands and some parts of Java, and is a common prey species for jackals, foxes and birds of prey.

children.name: Lepus oiostolus
children.description: The woolly hare inhabits the Tibetan Plateau and adjacent high-altitude grasslands of China, Nepal and India, typically above 3,500 metres and up to 5,500 metres — among the highest-elevation hares in the world. It has a dense, soft, woolly pelage that insulates against the extreme cold of Tibetan winters. A large, robust hare, it is an important prey species for Tibetan foxes and upland raptors.

children.name: Lepus othus
children.description: The Alaskan hare is the largest hare in North America — sometimes exceeding 5 kg — inhabiting coastal and lowland tundra of western and southwestern Alaska. Unlike the Arctic hare, it remains essentially solitary and does not form large aggregations. It is white year-round in the most northerly parts of its range, turning grey-brown in summer at lower latitudes.

children.name: Lepus peguensis
children.description: The Burmese hare occupies open grassland, scrubland and cultivated areas of Myanmar, Thailand, Cambodia, Laos and southern China. It is a small, pale-ochre hare with a rufous nape and relatively long ears. Its association with disturbed agricultural habitats has allowed it to persist despite widespread deforestation in mainland Southeast Asia.

children.name: Lepus saxatilis
children.description: The scrub hare is one of the most familiar hares of southern Africa, widespread from South Africa north to Zimbabwe and Mozambique in savanna, open woodland and scrubland. It is a large hare with grizzled grey-brown fur and white undersides, most active at night when it grazes on grasses and forbs. Its populations support important predator communities including leopards, caracals and large owls.

children.name: Lepus sinensis
children.description: The Chinese hare inhabits open grassland, shrubland and forest edge in southern China, Taiwan and the Korean Peninsula, with records extending into northern Vietnam. It is a relatively small, rufous-brown hare with large eyes and moderate ears. On Taiwan it is the only native hare and is considered a distinct subspecies by some authorities.

children.name: Lepus starcki
children.description: The Ethiopian hare is endemic to the upland grasslands and heathlands of the Ethiopian Highlands, where it typically inhabits high-elevation Afroalpine habitats above 3,000 metres. It is a medium-sized hare with grizzled brown-ochre pelage and has long been confused with the Cape hare. Recent studies support its recognition as a full species based on morphological and genetic distinctiveness.

children.name: Lepus tolai
children.description: The Tolai hare ranges across the steppes, semi-deserts and desert margins of Central Asia from the Caspian region through Kazakhstan, Mongolia and northern China. It is well adapted to arid conditions, with pale sandy-buff pelage providing camouflage against steppe soils and large ears for thermoregulation. It is an important prey species for steppe eagles, corsac foxes and wolves.

children.name: Lepus townsendii
children.description: The white-tailed jackrabbit is the largest North American jackrabbit, inhabiting open prairies, shrub-steppes and alpine meadows of western and central North America. Unlike the black-tailed jackrabbit it turns white or pale grey in winter, providing camouflage against snow. It is an important prey species for golden eagles and is sensitive to the intensification of agriculture across the Great Plains.

children.name: Lepus victoriae
children.description: Lepus victoriae is a medium-sized hare of East African grasslands and savannas, ranging from Uganda and Kenya south to Tanzania and western Zambia, frequently found near Lake Victoria from which its name derives. It inhabits open wooded grassland, cultivated areas and the margins of wetlands. It is one of several closely related African hares that have been shuffled between species boundaries as taxonomy has been revised.

children.name: Lepus yarkandensis
children.description: The Yarkand hare is endemic to the riparian thickets and agricultural oases of the Tarim Basin in Xinjiang, China — one of the most arid regions on Earth. It is a small, pale, long-eared hare adapted to life in desert river corridors dominated by tamarisk and Euphrates poplar. It is assessed as Near Threatened by the IUCN due to the degradation and loss of riparian forests from water diversion for irrigation.

children.name: Lepus tibetanus
children.description: The desert hare inhabits sandy and rocky deserts, semi-arid steppes and gravel plains across the Gobi and Taklimakan deserts of China and Mongolia, extending into northwestern India. It is a large, pale hare with long white-tipped ears and buff-grey fur that blends with desert substrates. It is largely nocturnal and depends on sparse shrubby vegetation for both food and shelter in extreme desert environments.

children.name: Lepus meiguensis
children.description: The Meigu hare is a recently described species known from a restricted area of Sichuan Province in southwestern China, discovered through morphological and molecular analyses. It inhabits alpine meadows and shrubland at high elevations in the Daxue Mountains. It is considered Data Deficient by the IUCN and requires further survey work to determine its true range and conservation status.

children.name: Lepus baicalensis
children.description: The Baikal hare is distributed across the forested and shrubby margins of Lake Baikal and the broader steppe and forest-steppe zone of Transbaikalia in Russia and adjacent Mongolia. It occupies a transitional zone between the forested mountain hare and the open-steppe Tolai hare and has sometimes been treated as a subspecies of the latter. It is characterised by a patchy seasonal moult and mixed forested-steppe ecology.

children.name: Lepus whitakeri
children.description: Whitaker's hare is a poorly known species of North Africa, restricted to coastal areas of Tunisia, Libya and possibly adjacent Algeria. It inhabits sandy coastal plains, dunes and low scrub near the Mediterranean coast. Long treated as a subspecies of the Cape hare, genetic and morphological evidence supports its recognition as a full species.

children.name: Lepus coreanus
children.description: The Korean hare is endemic to the Korean Peninsula, inhabiting temperate forests, scrubland and the margins of cultivated land from sea level to montane elevations. It is closely related to the Chinese and Manchurian hares and was long treated as a subspecies of one of them. Like other hares in the region it is nocturnal and solitary, foraging at night on grasses, bark and agricultural crops.

children.name: Lepus halli
children.description: Hall's hare was described from specimens collected in the grasslands and shrublands of central Mexico. It is closely related to the black-tailed jackrabbit and has at times been treated as its subspecies. Its taxonomy remains under active review and it is classified as Data Deficient by the IUCN pending further morphological and genetic analysis.

children.name: Lepus oaxacensis
children.description: The Oaxacan jackrabbit is restricted to dry grassland, scrub and open savanna in the Tehuantepec region and Pacific slopes of Oaxaca and Chiapas, Mexico. It is closely related to the black-tailed jackrabbit and distinguished by subtle morphological and genetic characters. Its restricted range in an area of rapid agricultural change makes it a species of conservation concern.

children.name: Lepus kunmingensis
children.description: The Kunming hare is known from the Kunming Basin and adjacent highlands of Yunnan Province, China, where it inhabits cultivated fields, grasslands and open shrubland at middle elevations. It was described from specimens collected around Kunming city. It is closely related to, and sometimes confused with, the Yunnan hare Lepus comus, and its taxonomic boundaries are still being clarified.

children.name: Lepus butleri
children.description: The Uganda hare is a little-known species from the savanna and woodland zones of Uganda and adjacent parts of South Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Described on the basis of limited museum specimens, it has been variously synonymised with the African savanna hare or Scrub hare before being maintained as a distinct species by recent authorities. Field surveys of its ecology and distribution remain sparse.

children.name: Lepus whytei
children.description: Whyte's hare is recorded from the Nyika Plateau and montane grasslands of Malawi and adjacent Zambia, inhabiting high-altitude grassland and heathland above 1,800 metres. It is a medium-sized, grizzled-brown hare that has been shuffled between species and subspecies status within the African hare complex. Its montane distribution suggests it is likely reproductively isolated from the lowland populations of related species.

children.name: Lepus starki
children.description: Stark's hare is a little-known African species from the savanna and semi-arid zones of Kenya and northern Tanzania, closely allied to the Cape hare. It inhabits open dry grassland and Acacia bushland. Its taxonomic identity has been debated and it has sometimes been treated as a synonym of Lepus capensis, though genetic evidence suggests it is a distinct lineage.

children.name: Lepus schlumbergeri
children.description: Schlumberger's hare is a poorly known species from the semi-arid lowlands of Somalia and the Ogaden region of Ethiopia, inhabiting dry scrub and open bushed grassland. It is often regarded as part of the habessinicus complex or as a synonym of L. habessinicus, though some authorities maintain its distinctiveness. Like most Somali region leporids it is poorly surveyed.

children.name: Lepus zulu
children.description: The Natal scrub hare is a recently recognised species from the eastern seaboard of South Africa, separated from the main scrub hare on the basis of morphological and molecular differences. It inhabits dense coastal thicket, shrubby vegetation and the margins of forest in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga. Its relationship to the broader Lepus saxatilis complex continues to be refined as genetic data accumulates.

children.name: Lepus somalicus
children.description: The Somali hare is found in the arid plains, semi-desert and dry thorn scrub of Somalia, Djibouti and the Ogaden region of Ethiopia. It is a pale, large-eared hare well adapted to hot, arid conditions. Its taxonomic status vis-à-vis the Cape hare and Abyssinian hare has been disputed, but recent morphological work supports its recognition as a distinct species.

children.name: Lepus crawshayi
children.description: Crawshay's hare inhabits miombo woodland, dambos and open grassland in Zambia, Malawi, Mozambique and Tanzania. It is a medium-sized hare with grizzled brown-grey pelage and relatively large ears. Long treated as part of the Lepus capensis or Lepus microtis complex, modern taxonomy recognises it as a distinct species within the diverse assemblage of sub-Saharan African hares.

children.name: Lepus kabylicus
children.description: The Algerian hare is endemic to northern Algeria, inhabiting the Atlas Mountains and adjacent coastal ranges in scrubland, garrigue and open forest. It was described in the 19th century and was long subsumed within the Cape hare, but is now treated as a distinct Maghrebian species. It faces increasing pressure from agricultural development and hunting in its restricted range.

children.name: Lepus aegyptius
children.description: The Egyptian hare is a widespread species of the Nile Valley, delta and adjacent semi-desert regions of Egypt and the Sinai Peninsula, extending into parts of Sudan and Libya. It is a medium-sized, pale sandy hare adapted to hot and arid lowland conditions. It has historically been viewed as a subspecies of the Cape hare, but is now recognised as distinct based on its distinctive morphology and genetics.

children.name: Lepus arabicus
children.description: The Arabian hare is native to the Arabian Peninsula including Saudi Arabia, Oman, Yemen and the United Arab Emirates, inhabiting gravel desert, sandy plains and scrub at low to mid elevations. It is a small, pale hare with large ears for heat dissipation in the extreme heat of the Arabian Desert. Its taxonomy relative to the Cape hare is still debated, with some authorities treating it as a subspecies of Lepus capensis.

children.name: Lepus omanensis
children.description: The Omani hare is a small to medium-sized hare endemic to the mountains and adjacent plains of Oman and the UAE, inhabiting wadis, rocky hillsides and shrubby desert. It has been recognised as distinct from the Arabian hare on the basis of its smaller size and isolated montane distribution in the Hajar Mountains. It is assessed as Least Concern by the IUCN but remains poorly surveyed.

children.name: Lepus nicodemus
children.description: Nicodemus's hare is a little-known species from the Great Lakes region of east-central Africa, recorded from Rwanda, Burundi and adjacent DRC. It inhabits highland grasslands, moorlands and agricultural margins at elevations above 1,500 metres. Its taxonomic distinctiveness from adjacent species in the Cape hare complex has been upheld by recent genetic analyses.

children.name: Lepus pestifer
children.description: Lepus pestifer is a hare of the high-altitude meadows and alpine shrublands of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau transitional zone in western China, where it can reach very high densities in productive grassland. It has been treated as a subspecies of the woolly hare or Tibetan hare but is recognised as a distinct species by Chinese mammalogists. Its population dynamics and habitat requirements on the Plateau remain under investigation.

children.name: Lepus dayanus
children.description: The Pakistan hare inhabits the dry scrubland, stony plains and agricultural areas of Pakistan, northwestern India and eastern Afghanistan. Long treated as a subspecies of the Indian hare Lepus nigricollis, it differs in pelage colour, morphometry and ecology and is recognised as a distinct species by recent authorities. It is the most common large mammal in many arid agricultural landscapes of Pakistan.

children.name: Lepus ferghanensis
children.description: The Ferghana hare is native to the Ferghana Valley and adjacent mountain foothills of Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan, where it inhabits the scrubby steppe and lower mountain slopes of this densely populated Central Asian valley. It is closely related to the Tolai hare and has been treated as its subspecies, but morphological and distributional evidence supports species-level recognition. It faces pressure from intensive agriculture in the Ferghana Valley.

children.name: Lepus pamirensis
children.description: The Pamir hare inhabits the cold high-altitude desert and alpine meadow zone of the Pamir and Karakoram ranges in Tajikistan, Afghanistan and northern Pakistan above 3,500 metres. It has generally been treated as a subspecies of the desert hare Lepus tibetanus but is geographically isolated and morphologically distinct. Its small range and high-altitude specialisation make it potentially vulnerable to climate change.

children.name: Lepus ochropus
children.description: The ochre-footed hare is recorded from the wooded grasslands and miombo of Tanzania and northern Mozambique, characterised by its pale ochre-coloured legs against a greyer body. It has been variably placed as a subspecies of the savanna hare or as a distinct species, with most modern treatments accepting its specific distinctness. It is patchily distributed and little studied in the field.

children.name: Lepus mediterraneus
children.description: The Mediterranean hare inhabits open habitats of the central and eastern Mediterranean, including parts of Italy, the Balkans and Anatolia, in scrubland, garrigue and agricultural land. It is closely related to the European hare but adapted to warmer, drier Mediterranean conditions with a more compact build. Some authorities synonymise it with Lepus europaeus, but genetic studies suggest distinct Mediterranean lineages.

children.name: Lepus isabelinus
children.description: The Persian hare is distributed across the semi-arid plateaux, stony plains and shrubby steppes of Iran, Afghanistan and adjacent Turkmenistan. It is a medium-to-large pale hare with a sandy-buff dorsum and white undersides, blending well with the arid substrates of the Iranian Plateau. It is assessed as Least Concern by the IUCN and is relatively common in appropriate steppe habitats.

children.name: Lepus keniensis
children.description: The Kenya hare is restricted to the highland grasslands and montane heaths of the Kenyan Highlands and the Aberdare Mountains, at elevations generally above 2,000 metres. It is a medium-sized hare with grizzled brown-grey fur and relatively large ears. It has been treated as a subspecies of the African savanna hare but is recognised as distinct based on its ecology and montane isolation.

children.name: Sylvilagus
children.description: The cottontail rabbits, around 19 species distributed across the Americas from Alaska to Argentina, named for their distinctive fluffy white tail and occupying a wide range of habitats from desert to swamp.

children.name: Sylvilagus floridanus
children.description: The eastern cottontail is the most abundant and widely distributed rabbit in North America, occupying shrubby edges, old fields, forest margins and suburban gardens from southern Canada to Venezuela. It relies on dense brushy cover for escape from predators and does not burrow, nesting instead in shallow scrapes lined with grass and fur. As a highly prolific prey species it supports a wide array of predators from red foxes to red-tailed hawks.

children.name: Sylvilagus audubonii
children.description: The desert cottontail inhabits arid and semi-arid shrublands, grasslands and desert scrub of the Great Basin, Mojave and Sonoran deserts of the western United States and Mexico. It is active at dawn and dusk and during cooler nighttime hours, resting in shaded burrows during the heat of the day. It is one of the most important prey species for coyotes, kit foxes and rattlesnakes in desert ecosystems.

children.name: Sylvilagus bachmani
children.description: The brush rabbit is a small, dark cottontail restricted to the chaparral, coastal scrub and dense brushy habitats of the Pacific coast from Oregon to Baja California. It is strongly associated with dense cover and rarely ventures far from brush, relying on proximity to escape vegetation rather than speed. The riparian subspecies S. b. riparius of the San Joaquin Valley is critically endangered.

children.name: Sylvilagus aquaticus
children.description: The swamp rabbit is the largest cottontail in North America, inhabiting bottomland hardwood forests, swamps, marshes and river floodplains across the south-central United States. It is an adept swimmer and readily enters water to escape predators or move between habitat patches. Its declining populations are closely tied to the loss and fragmentation of bottomland hardwood forest habitats in the lower Mississippi Valley.

children.name: Sylvilagus brasiliensis
children.description: The forest cottontail has one of the widest distributions of any New World rabbit, ranging from Mexico south through Central America and into South America as far as northern Argentina. It inhabits the edges of tropical and subtropical forests, dense brush and secondary vegetation at elevations up to 4,800 metres in the Andes. It is a small, dark rabbit adapted to humid forest environments unlike the open-habitat affinities of most other cottontails.

children.name: Sylvilagus cognatus
children.description: The Manzano mountain cottontail is restricted to the Manzano and Sandia Mountains of central New Mexico, USA. It inhabits dense shrubby vegetation, oak scrub and rocky slopes at middle to upper elevations. Long considered a subspecies of the robust cottontail, it is recognised as a distinct species based on its isolated distribution and morphological differences.

children.name: Sylvilagus cunicularius
children.description: The Mexican cottontail is one of the largest cottontails, endemic to the highlands and volcanic slopes of central and southern Mexico, particularly around the Transvolcanic Belt. It inhabits grassland, shrubland and open pine-oak forest at elevations from 1,500 to 4,000 metres. It is an important prey species in Mexican highland ecosystems, hunted by coyotes, golden eagles and mountain lions.

children.name: Sylvilagus dicei
children.description: Dice's cottontail is a small, poorly known rabbit restricted to the mountains of Costa Rica and western Panama at elevations above 1,500 metres. It inhabits paramo, shrubby highland forest margins and secondary forest at high altitudes. Its limited range and highland specialisation make it potentially vulnerable to climate change and habitat alteration.

children.name: Sylvilagus graysoni
children.description: The Tres Marías cottontail is endemic to the Tres Marías Islands off the coast of Nayarit, Mexico in the Pacific Ocean. It is a medium-sized cottontail restricted to the tropical deciduous forests of this small island archipelago. Assessed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, it faces threats from habitat degradation and predation by introduced animals.

children.name: Sylvilagus hirsutus
children.description: The Omiltemi cottontail is an extremely range-restricted species known only from the cloud forest of the Sierra Madre del Sur near Omiltemi in Guerrero, Mexico. It is assessed as Endangered by the IUCN owing to its tiny known range, ongoing deforestation and the general rarity of cloud forest in this part of Mexico. It was described in 1904 and very few specimens have been collected since.

children.name: Sylvilagus insonus
children.description: The Insonus cottontail — also called the Antequerense rabbit or Tapeti — is restricted to pine-oak forests and brushy slopes in Oaxaca, Mexico. It was long treated as a subspecies of the Mexican cottontail but morphological analysis supports its distinctiveness. Very little ecological or behavioural data exists for this species, which remains poorly known.

children.name: Sylvilagus mansuetus
children.description: The San José brush rabbit is endemic to San José Island in the Gulf of California, Baja California Sur, Mexico. It is a small, insular cottontail related to the brush rabbit and inhabits the sparse desert shrubland of this arid island. Like other island endemics it is assessed as Vulnerable given its tiny range and the potential for catastrophic events or introduced predators to eliminate the population.

children.name: Sylvilagus nuttallii
children.description: The mountain cottontail inhabits the sagebrush steppes, mountain shrublands and coniferous forest margins of the intermountain west, from southern Canada through the Rocky Mountain states to the Mojave. It is closely associated with sagebrush and is more tolerant of cold and elevation than most cottontails. Rocky outcrops and dense brush provide essential shelter from the coyotes, badgers and raptors that prey on it.

children.name: Sylvilagus obscurus
children.description: The Appalachian cottontail is a small, dark cottontail restricted to high-elevation heath balds, shrubby clearings and coniferous forests of the central and southern Appalachian Mountains of the eastern United States. Long confused with the New England cottontail, it was formally recognised as a distinct species in 1992. It is sensitive to forest maturation and depends on areas of dense, low shrubby vegetation.

children.name: Sylvilagus palustris
children.description: The marsh rabbit is a semi-aquatic cottontail inhabiting freshwater and brackish marshes, wet meadows and swampy thickets along the Atlantic and Gulf coastal plains of the southeastern United States. It is a strong swimmer and diver, regularly entering water to forage and escape predators. The Lower Keys subspecies S. p. hefneri, restricted to a few islands in the Florida Keys, is listed as Endangered.

children.name: Sylvilagus robustus
children.description: The Davis Mountains cottontail is restricted to the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains of west Texas and possibly adjacent Coahuila, Mexico. It is the largest of the rocky mountain cottontails, inhabiting shrubby rocky slopes and canyon brushland at elevations of 1,500–2,500 metres. It was long treated as a subspecies of the eastern cottontail and robust cottontail before being recognised as a distinct species.

children.name: Sylvilagus transitionalis
children.description: The New England cottontail is the only cottontail native to the northeastern United States, now restricted to fragmented patches of young forest, shrubby growth and thicket from southern Maine to New York. It has declined dramatically from habitat loss as forests have matured, displacing the dense shrubby early-successional habitat it requires. Targeted conservation programmes are actively creating and managing habitat to recover this Species of Greatest Conservation Need.

children.name: Sylvilagus varynaensis
children.description: The Varinas cottontail is known from a limited area of the llanos grasslands of Barinas state in western Venezuela. Described in 1980, it is one of the least-known South American leporids and has rarely been recorded since its original description. Its taxonomic status and population size remain uncertain, and it may be more widespread in Venezuelan lowland savanna than current records suggest.

children.name: Sylvilagus veraecrucis
children.description: The Veracruz cottontail is a poorly known species endemic to the humid tropical lowlands of Veracruz state on Mexico's Gulf coast. It inhabits dense tropical scrub and the margins of tropical forests in an area subject to extensive agricultural development. It is assessed as Near Threatened by the IUCN given ongoing habitat loss in the lowland tropics of eastern Mexico.

children.name: Pentalagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Amami rabbit, a primitive and endangered species endemic to two Japanese islands, notable for its small eyes, short ears, and dark pelage — a 'living fossil' among leporids.

children.name: Pentalagus furnessi
children.description: The Amami rabbit is a relict species restricted to Amami-Oshima and Tokunoshima in the Ryukyu Islands of Japan, where it inhabits dense subtropical forest. Unlike most leporids it has short, rounded ears, small eyes and dark brown-black fur — features considered primitive among rabbits and earning it the epithet 'living fossil'. It is assessed as Endangered by the IUCN; introduced mongooses and feral cats are its main threats.

children.name: Nesolagus
children.description: The striped rabbits, a small genus of two species from Southeast Asia characterised by striking dark-striped pelage — among the most visually distinctive of all leporids.

children.name: Nesolagus netscheri
children.description: The Sumatran striped rabbit is endemic to the montane forests of Sumatra, Indonesia, where it inhabits dense rainforest undergrowth and bamboo thickets between 600 and 1,400 metres elevation. It is marked with bold dark brown stripes on a pale buff background, making it unmistakeable among leporids. Assessed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, it is threatened by extensive deforestation of Sumatran mountain forests.

children.name: Nesolagus timminsi
children.description: The Annamite striped rabbit was not described until 1999, discovered from specimens in Lao and Vietnamese markets along the Annamite Mountains. Like its Sumatran relative it bears bold dark stripes, converging on the rump and flanks. It inhabits the dense evergreen forests of the Annamite Mountains straddling the Laos–Vietnam border and is assessed as Endangered due to intensive hunting and habitat loss.

children.name: Romerolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the volcano rabbit, the world's second smallest rabbit, endemic to four volcanoes near Mexico City and representing one of the most geographically restricted mammals in North America.

children.name: Romerolagus diazi
children.description: The volcano rabbit — locally known as the zacatuche — is the world's second smallest rabbit and one of the most endangered, restricted to the bunchgrass-covered slopes of four volcanoes south of Mexico City: Popocatépetl, Iztaccíhuatl, Tláloc and Pelado. It has extremely short ears, a barely visible tail and emits high-pitched calls rather than thumping to communicate. Assessed as Endangered by the IUCN, its specialised habitat is threatened by urban expansion and grazing.

children.name: Caprolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the hispid hare, a highly threatened species from the South Asian foothills characterised by coarse, bristly fur unlike any other leporid.

children.name: Caprolagus hispidus
children.description: The hispid hare, also called the Assam rabbit, is a large, coarse-furred leporid restricted to the tall grasslands — 'elephant grass' — of the Himalayan foothills in northern India, Nepal and possibly Bhutan. Its stiff, dark brown bristly pelage is unique among hares and rabbits. Assessed as Endangered by the IUCN, populations have declined severely due to the conversion of tall grasslands to agriculture and annual burning.

children.name: Poelagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Bunyoro rabbit, a central African species that fills an ecological niche similar to European rabbits in the savanna-forest mosaic of equatorial Africa.

children.name: Poelagus marjorita
children.description: The Bunyoro rabbit inhabits rocky outcrops, dense scrub and grassland-forest margins from Sudan and the Central African Republic east to Uganda and northern Tanzania. Unlike most African leporids it is strongly associated with rocky terrain where it shelters in rock crevices and dense vegetation. It is assessed as Least Concern but is poorly known and probably under-recorded across its range.

children.name: Pronolagus
children.description: The red rock hares, four species endemic to southern and eastern Africa, all characterised by rufous-red pelage and a close association with rocky habitat.

children.name: Pronolagus crassicaudatus
children.description: The greater red rock hare is the largest of the rock hares, inhabiting rocky hillsides, boulder fields and koppies in the eastern escarpment zone from KwaZulu-Natal north to southern Zimbabwe and Mozambique. Its rich rufous-brown pelage and bushy tail are distinctive in the field. It is strictly nocturnal, sheltering by day in rock crevices, and is an important prey species for Verreaux's eagles.

children.name: Pronolagus randensis
children.description: Jameson's red rock hare occurs on rocky hillsides, granite outcrops and escarpments from South Africa's Limpopo Province north through Zimbabwe and Zambia to Malawi. It is medium-sized with bright rufous fur and a characteristically bushy tail. Like other rock hares it is nocturnal, rarely straying far from its rocky refuge habitat, and feeds on grasses and forbs in the vicinity of boulders.

children.name: Pronolagus rupestris
children.description: Smith's red rock hare has the widest distribution of the Pronolagus species, occurring on rocky terrain throughout much of southern Africa from the Western Cape north to central Zimbabwe. It favours loose rocky slopes, koppies and boulder-strewn hillsides where it hides in crevices and under overhangs during the day. Its rufous pelage is somewhat paler and more variable than its congeners.

children.name: Pronolagus saundersiae
children.description: Hewitt's red rock hare is the least-known of the four rock hares, restricted to a narrow zone of rocky escarpment in the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal of South Africa. It was long synonymised with Smith's red rock hare before being re-elevated to full species status based on its smaller size and genetic distinctiveness. Relatively little is known of its ecology, and it is assessed as Near Threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Brachylagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pygmy rabbit, the smallest leporid in North America, uniquely associated with tall sagebrush in the Great Basin.

children.name: Brachylagus idahoensis
children.description: The pygmy rabbit is a rabbit species native to the United States. It is one of the only two native rabbit species in North America to dig its own burrow. The pygmy rabbit differs significantly from species within either the Lepus (hare) or Sylvilagus (cottontail) genera, and was once considered to be within the genus Brachylagus, which includes one extinct species.

children.name: Bunolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the riverine rabbit, one of the world's most critically endangered mammals, restricted to the central Karoo of South Africa.

children.name: Bunolagus monticularis
children.description: The riverine rabbit, also known as the bushman rabbit or bushman hare, is a species of rabbit that lives among patches of thick vegetation in the Karoo of South Africa's Western and Northern Cape provinces. It is the only member of the genus Bunolagus. It is classified a critically endangered species by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN); the most recent estimates of the species' population range from 157 to 207 mature individuals, and 224 to 380 total.
